nativeRange.habitat: Shallow, slow-moving blackwater streams and forest pools over sandy or leaf-litter substrate; heavily shaded by overhanging vegetation; water stained dark brown by tannins from decaying organic matter

decorPreferences: dense leaf litter (Indian almond / Catappa leaves) to acidify and tannin-stain water
driftwood and twisted roots for cover
dim lighting or floating plants to diffuse light
gentle filtration (sponge filter) — avoids strong current
peat filtration or blackwater extract to replicate natural chemistry

diet.notes: Micropredator in the wild, feeding on tiny invertebrates, zooplankton, and small insects. In captivity accepts high-quality micro-foods: Cyclops, micro-worms, baby brine shrimp (newly hatched), Daphnia nauplii, and ultra-fine crushed flake or micro-pellets (≤0.3 mm). Belly shape should appear slightly rounded; sunken belly indicates underfeeding. Feed 2–3 small portions daily.

goodWith: other Sundadanio species (where pure species identity is confirmed)
small peaceful nano fish tolerant of acidic water (Boraras species, Trigonostigma espei)
blackwater-adapted Corydoras pygmaeus or C. habrosus
small peaceful dwarf shrimp (Neocaridina may be stressed by low pH; Caridina species better matched)
avoidWith: any fish large enough to eat 2 cm fish
boisterous or fast-swimming species that outcompete at feeding
fish requiring alkaline or hard water

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species page image for Sundadanio axelrodi; verify current availability. Small specimen photograph, good color representation.
Verify this filename is correct on Wikimedia; may show male color form. Check attribution before use.

summary: A tiny (≤2 cm) blackwater specialist from Sumatra and Borneo with iridescent blue flanks, not a true rasbora despite its trade name. Demands very soft, acidic water (pH 4.5–6.5) and is poorly suited to community tanks — rated advanced due to strict water chemistry requirements and sensitivity to suboptimal conditions.
careNotes:
The Blue Neon Rasbora is one of the most demanding nano fish in the freshwater hobby. Despite the trade name, Sundadanio axelrodi is not a rasbora at all — the genus Sundadanio was separated from Cyprinidae and placed in its own family Sundadanionidae following molecular revision by Conway et al. (2012). Critically, what is sold in the trade as 'S. axelrodi' is frequently one of several cryptic species in the Sundadanio complex (at least 7 named species including S. atomus, S. gargula, S. margarition, S. retiarius, S. rohani, and S. indonesicus); most aquarists and vendors cannot distinguish them visually. Care parameters below apply broadly across the complex.

Water chemistry is the paramount challenge. These fish originate from extreme blackwater environments — pH as low as 3.5 has been recorded in some collection sites — and they are not adaptable to neutral or alkaline tap water. Target pH 4.5–6.0, total hardness (gH) 1–3 dH, and near-zero carbonate hardness (kH 0–2). Reverse-osmosis water remineralized to very low GH, combined with peat filtration or blackwater extracts (Catappa / Indian almond leaves, alder cones), is the recommended approach. Sudden pH shifts are lethal; stabilize the tank for weeks before introducing fish. Water temperature 24–27°C is ideal.

Because of their tiny size and delicate constitution, Sundadanio species are best maintained in a species-only or carefully curated blackwater biotope tank. Tank volume of 40–60 L is workable; a longer footprint (60+ cm) is preferred over a tall tank for this midwater shoaler. Keep in groups of 8 or more — lone or small-group specimens are visibly stressed and fade in color. Use only a gentle sponge filter; power filters create excessive flow and turbulence for these tiny fish. Dim lighting with floating plants enhances both fish comfort and color display.

Food particle size is critical: these fish have tiny mouths and will ignore anything they cannot fit. Newly hatched baby brine shrimp (BBS), Cyclops, micro-worms, and Daphnia nauplii are staple live/frozen foods. Dry foods must be ultra-fine (micro-pellets, powdered flake). Feed 2–3 times daily in small amounts.

Mortality in the first weeks post-purchase is common, attributed to stress from transit, brief exposure to hard/alkaline retail water, and parasitic load. Quarantine in a pre-cycled, properly acidic quarantine tank before adding to a display. Avoid copper-based medications in any tank housing Sundadanio.

See also related species: Boraras brigittae (Chili Rasbora), Boraras maculatus — compatible blackwater nano fish with similar care requirements.
breedingNotes: Breeding in captivity is possible but uncommon and seldom reported outside specialist circles. Sundadanio species are egg-scatterers with no parental care; fine-leaved plants (Java moss, hornwort) or a spawning mop are used to collect adhesive eggs. Spawning is triggered by very soft acidic water and gradual temperature drops simulating rain season (22–24°C). Eggs are tiny and sensitive to fungal infection; methylene blue in low dose or Java moss surface chemistry helps. Fry are microscopic at hatching and require infusoria or commercial paramecia culture for the first 5–7 days before accepting nauplii. Breeding difficulty is rated advanced due to the exacting water chemistry required and fragile fry.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Sundadanio axelrodi
primary.notes: Primary source for husbandry parameters, taxonomy, native range, and species complex notes. Provided pH 4.0–6.5, temperature 20–26°C, hardness 18–90 ppm (≈1–5 dH), adult size ~2 cm, blackwater biotope, Sumatra/Borneo range, and discussion of the Conway et al. 2012 Sundadanionidae placement and cryptic species complex.

additional.name: FishBase — Sundadanio axelrodi
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (2.0 cm TL), taxonomy (Sundadanionidae, Cypriniformes), and native range (Sumatra, Borneo — Indonesia and Malaysia). Confirmed planktivore diet classification and blackwater habitat.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Nano Fish and Blackwater Species
additional.notes: Supplementary trade context for Sundadanio species; confirmed nano classification, advanced care rating, and micro-food requirements. Aquarium Co-Op carries Sundadanio species seasonally and notes pH 4.5–6.5 and blackwater setup as requirements.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Sundadanio axelrodi species profile
additional.notes: Supplementary care profile; confirmed cryptic species complex caveat (fish sold as S. axelrodi often other Sundadanio spp.), advanced care difficulty, group size requirement (8+), and feeding with micro-foods. Noted that wild collection sites often have pH below 5.0.

additional.name: Conway K.W. et al. (2012) — Revision of Sundadanio and description of six new species (Cypriniformes: Sundadanionidae)
additional.notes: Scientific basis for Sundadanionidae family placement and description of 7 species in the S. axelrodi complex (S. axelrodi, S. atomus, S. gargula, S. margarition, S. retiarius, S. rohani, S. indonesicus). Essential context for the trade-name ambiguity caveat.

fish.breedingNotes: Egg-scatterer; requires extremely soft acidic water (pH 4.5–5.5, gH <2) to spawn. Fine-leaved plants or moss provide egg attachment sites. Fry require infusoria initially; seldom bred commercially due to exacting conditions.
